Basics.title: Improving Retrieval-Augmented Generation through Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
Basics.code_availability: publicly available (https://github.com/chenyiqun/MMOA-RAG). Prompt templates for each agent are described in Appendix B but not released as standalone files. Trained model weights are not released.

Core.one_sentence_thesis: MMOA-RAG trains all components of a question-answering system (query improver, document picker, answer writer) as a cooperative team sharing the same goal—getting the right answer—rather than optimizing each piece separately.

core_problem.problem_statement: RAG pipelines are typically optimized module-by-module via supervised fine-tuning, causing misalignment: improving one component's individual metric (e.g., retrieval relevance) does not guarantee better final answers.
why_it_matters: Real-world impact: Better coordinated RAG systems can significantly improve AI assistants, search engines, and knowledge-based applications used by millions

End-to-end QA correctness depends on interdependent module behaviors—optimizing each module in isolation means the query rewriter might produce reformulations that help retrieval but confuse the answer generator, or the selector might pick documents that look relevant individually but lack the combination of facts needed for multi-hop reasoning

Prior RL-based methods (Rewrite-Retrieve-Read, BGM) optimize only one or two modules with PPO (a single-agent RL algorithm), missing the full multi-module coordination that MMOA-RAG addresses through multi-agent RL
core_problem.concrete_example: When asked 'What conference was the Vermont Catamounts men's soccer team's conference formerly known as from 1988 to 1996?', an independently-trained query rewriter produces only one sub-question retrieving football documents, missing the conference history. The paper's case study (Appendix I) shows MMOA-RAG fixing this by decomposing into two coordinated sub-questions that retrieve both the team's conference affiliation and its historical name.

key_novelty.main_idea: Cooperative Multi-Agent RL for Modular RAG (MMOA-RAG)
explanation: Model each RAG module (Query Rewriter, Document Selector, Answer Generator) as an RL agent, all sharing the same LLM backbone with different prompts, and trained to maximize a shared reward (F1 score of the final answer)
Use MAPPO (a multi-agent extension of PPO) to jointly optimize all modules so they learn to coordinate—like training a relay team for overall race time rather than individual leg times
The technical formulation—shared reward structure, per-agent penalty terms, and MAPPO training loop—is detailed in Technical_details below

evaluation_highlights: +1.8 to +2.7 F1 improvement over best baselines across 3 QA benchmarks (Table 1)
MAPPO joint training adds +3.6 F1 over SFT warm-start on HotpotQA, demonstrating coordination is learned (Table 2)
Generalizes across retrieval backends (Contriever, BGE, E5) and out-of-domain datasets

breakthrough_assessment.justification: Novel application of multi-agent RL to RAG with consistent empirical gains across benchmarks. First to demonstrate cooperative learning across 3 RAG modules. Improvements are meaningful (+1.8-2.7 F1) and the framework generalizes to different pipeline configurations. Accepted at NeurIPS 2025.

problem_definition.formal_setting: Open-domain question answering over document collections, including multi-hop and ambiguous questions
problem_definition.constraints_assumptions: Fixed retriever (Contriever), K=10 candidate documents per question, F1 score of final answer as shared reward

prerequisites: Basic understanding of language models (LLMs) and how they generate text
Basic knowledge of reinforcement learning concepts (rewards, agents, policies)

key_terms._comment: Define key terms upfront for accessibility. Every acronym and technical term used in this summary is defined here.
key_terms.RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation—AI systems that answer questions by first searching for relevant documents, then generating responses based on what they find
key_terms.MAPPO: Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization—a reinforcement learning algorithm that trains multiple AI components to cooperate by sharing feedback on overall performance
key_terms.PPO: Proximal Policy Optimization—a single-agent RL algorithm that updates a policy in small, stable steps using a clipped objective to prevent destructively large updates
key_terms.DPO: Direct Preference Optimization—a method that aligns language models to human preferences by directly optimizing on preference pairs rather than training a separate reward model
key_terms.MARL: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning—an RL setting where multiple agents learn policies simultaneously in a shared environment, as opposed to single-agent RL
key_terms.multi-hop QA: Questions requiring reasoning across multiple documents (e.g., 'Where was the director of Titanic born?' needs info about both the movie and the person)
key_terms.F1 score: A metric balancing precision (are answers correct?) and recall (are answers complete?), ranging from 0 to 100 in this paper
key_terms.EM: Exact Match—whether the predicted answer is exactly identical to the ground-truth answer (stricter than F1)
key_terms.SFT: Supervised Fine-Tuning—training a model on labeled examples before applying reinforcement learning, used here as a 'warm start' so agents can follow instructions before learning to cooperate
key_terms.dense retrieval: A method of finding relevant documents by comparing learned vector representations (embeddings) of queries and passages, as opposed to keyword matching (sparse retrieval)
key_terms.Contriever: A pre-trained dense retrieval model used in this paper as the default document retriever
key_terms.silver labels: Automatically generated training labels (as opposed to human-annotated 'gold' labels)—here, the Selector's SFT data uses heuristic relevance judgments rather than human annotations
key_terms.Actor-Critic: An RL architecture where one network (the Actor) decides what actions to take, and another (the Critic) estimates how good those actions are
key_terms.GAE: Generalized Advantage Estimation—a technique for estimating how much better an action was compared to the average, used to reduce variance in RL training
key_terms.KL divergence: A measure of how much a model's behavior has changed from a reference model; used here as a penalty to prevent agents from drifting too far from their SFT initialization
key_terms.clipped surrogate objective: The core PPO/MAPPO loss function that limits how much the policy can change in a single update step, ensuring stable training
key_terms.cosine schedule: A learning rate schedule that decreases the learning rate following a cosine curve from a maximum to a minimum value over training
key_terms.Top-k: A retrieval strategy that returns the k highest-scoring documents for a given query

modules.role: Decompose the initial question into one or more sub-questions for better retrieval coverage
modules.notes: RL agent; penalized if generating more than 4 sub-questions (P_QR = -0.5)

modules.role: Retrieve candidate documents from corpus for each sub-question
outputs: K=10 candidate documents D (top-5 per sub-question if 2 sub-questions)
modules.notes: Fixed component, treated as part of the environment—not an RL agent

modules.role: Filter candidate documents to select the subset most helpful for answering the question
inputs: Prompt_S + initial question q + K candidate documents D
modules.notes: RL agent; constrained action space (document IDs only); penalized for duplicate or malformed output (P_S = -1)

modules.role: Generate the final answer based on selected documents
inputs: Prompt_G + initial question q + selected documents D_selected
modules.notes: RL agent; penalized for excessively long answers (P_G = -0.5)

novel_architectural_elements: Multi-agent formulation of RAG pipeline: 3 agents (QR, S, G) share the same LLM but are distinguished by different prompts (parameter sharing)
Cooperative reward sharing: all agents receive the same F1 reward R_shared plus agent-specific penalty terms
Modular design allowing flexible configurations (QR+S+G, S+G, or QR+G)

model_architecture.base_model: Llama-3-8B-Instruct for all three agents (parameter sharing)

training_details.method: Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) with Actor-Critic architecture
objective_functions: Actor loss: PPO clipped surrogate objective (limits how much the policy changes per update) summed over all agents i and timesteps t
Critic loss: Clipped value function loss with GAE (Generalized Advantage Estimation) for stable advantage computation
Final reward includes KL penalty against SFT model: R = R_shared + P_i - β * log(π_θ/π_SFT)
training_details.warm_start: Each module warm-started via SFT on task-specific data: Query Rewriter uses Rewrite-Retrieve-Read data, Selector uses heuristic silver labels (automatically generated relevance judgments), Generator uses golden answers
training_details.adaptation: Full fine-tuning with parameter sharing across agents (same LLM, different prompts)
training_data_construction: SFT training data constructed per-module (see Appendix B of paper)

related_methods: RetRobust: Makes generator robust to irrelevant retrieved context via training with noisy data
Rewrite-Retrieve-Read: Trains a query rewrite module with PPO (single-agent RL), but does not jointly optimize selector or generator
BGM: Trains a bridge module between retriever and generator using PPO (single-agent RL), but does not jointly optimize other modules
Self-RAG: Uses special self-reflection tokens to decide when to retrieve and how to critique outputs
RAG-DDR: Uses DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) to align data preferences between RAG modules
key_differences: vs. RetRobust: Joint optimization of all 3 modules vs. only training generator for robustness
vs. Rewrite-Retrieve-Read: Simultaneous MAPPO optimization of QR+S+G vs. PPO on query rewriter only
vs. BGM: Optimizes QR+S+G together vs. PPO on bridge module only
vs. Self-RAG: Implicit coordination via shared MARL rewards vs. explicit self-reflection within single model

vs. RA-DIT (not cited in paper): RA-DIT uses dual instruction tuning to align retriever and generator, but tunes only two components sequentially rather than jointly optimizing three agents with shared reward

vs. RAFT (not cited in paper): RAFT fine-tunes a generator to be robust to distractor documents via chain-of-thought training, but does not optimize query rewriting or document selection—complementary to MMOA-RAG's multi-agent coordination
Novel contribution: First cooperative MARL approach treating RAG pipeline as multi-agent system

acknowledged_limitations: Training time scales linearly with number of agents (n agents = n× rollout and update time)
Focused on QA tasks only; not evaluated on summarization, dialogue, or other RAG applications
potential_issues: Parameter sharing across agents may limit specialization as number of agents grows
Requires ground truth answers for F1-based reward during training—not applicable to open-ended generation
Retriever is frozen; optimizing it jointly could yield further gains but is left for future work
scope_restrictions: Evaluated on 3 English QA datasets (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, AmbigQA)
Retriever limited to dense retrieval (Contriever, BGE, E5); not tested with sparse or generative retrieval

evaluation_setup.evaluation_setting: Open-domain question answering with retrieval from Wikipedia passages
evaluation_setup.statistical_methodology: Not explicitly reported in the paper

key_results.notes: Using Contriever retriever. Arithmetic check: 48.29 − 46.49 = 1.80 ✓. Rewrite-Retrieve-Read scored 46.32 F1.
Using Contriever retriever. Arithmetic check: 48.59 − 45.92 = 2.67 ✓. Other close baselines: RAG-DDR 45.83, BGM 45.76.
Shows benefit of MAPPO joint training over SFT alone. Arithmetic check: 48.29 − 44.69 = 3.60 ✓. Similar gains on other datasets.

key_results.baseline: Best baseline with BGE and E5 retrievers
key_results.delta: MMOA-RAG surpasses most baselines with both BGE and E5 retrievers
key_results.notes: Demonstrates generalization across retrieval backends. Exact values are in Appendix E, Table 7 of the paper.

main_takeaways: MMOA-RAG outperforms all baselines across all 3 metrics (Acc, EM, F1) on all 3 datasets (Table 1)
MAPPO joint training adds +2-4 F1 over SFT warm-start across different pipeline configurations (Table 2)
Ablation: Removing any single agent from joint optimization degrades performance; full QR+S+G is best (Figure 2)
Generalizes across retrievers (Contriever, BGE, E5) and out-of-domain datasets (Appendix E, F)

architecture_figure.what_it_shows: The overall framework of MMOA-RAG showing the four-module pipeline and MAPPO training loop—focuses on the core mechanism of cooperative multi-agent optimization
architecture_figure.visual_layout: Left-to-right flow: Input Question → Query Rewriter → Retriever → Selector → Generator → Predicted Answer, with a shared reward (F1) feedback loop flowing back from right to left
key_components_visible: Query Rewriter producing sub-questions from initial question (trainable RL agent, color-highlighted)
Retriever fetching candidate documents D for each sub-question (frozen component, visually distinct from trainable agents)
Selector filtering D to helpful subset D_selected (trainable RL agent, color-highlighted)
Generator producing final answer Ans_predict (trainable RL agent, color-highlighted)
Shared reward R_shared (F1 score) feeding back to all three trainable agents (shown with dashed reward arrows)
Per-agent penalty terms (P_QR, P_S, P_G) shown alongside shared reward
architecture_figure.reading_guide: Follow the pipeline left-to-right for inference flow. Solid arrows indicate data flow between modules. Dashed arrows show reward/gradient signals flowing back from the answer evaluation. The Retriever is visually distinguished (frozen, no reward arrows) from the three trainable agents. The yellow/highlighted section represents the shared reward computation. During training, the F1 score of the predicted answer is used as reward for all 3 agents via MAPPO.

major_experiment_figures.what_it_shows: Ablation results: F1 scores for MMOA-RAG vs. variants with each agent removed (w/o QR, w/o S, w/o G) across all 3 datasets
key_insights: Full MMOA-RAG (all 3 agents) achieves highest performance on all datasets
Removing Generator (w/o G) causes the largest drop, especially on single-hop AmbigQA
Removing Selector (w/o S) has the smallest impact, suggesting Generator can partially compensate

major_experiment_figures.what_it_shows: Training reward curves (F1 score) over training samples for MMOA-RAG and ablation variants on AmbigQA
key_insights: MMOA-RAG converges fastest and to the highest reward value
MMOA-RAG w/o G converges slowest, confirming Generator is the most critical agent for single-hop QA
